speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Eizenstein, you were telling us about your activities with the Partizaner group and the Volksdeutsche in--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were surrounded from all sides. And this lieutenant, from Finland, what he was a-- he was a sport instructor. He was very sophisticated, you know? He was a soldier from Finland, who survived Finland. That's the biggest loyals-- you know-- I don't know whatever you call them-- soldiers in the world. This was unbelievable things, you know, in Finland-- to fight Finland. And it got-- thank to God and him we survived. He give us survival. I, to survive, he kept us like kids. And he says, OK, guys, we got what we got. We got food. We got water. We got horses. We got everything we got. The only thing, he says, we have to get vehicles-- you understand-- to play Germans, and we go to Kiev. He knows the whole [NON-ENGLISH]. He knows the whole road I had to go-- to the bushes. And that's what we did. We got some vehicles, German vehicles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: --acquire these vehicles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we got them, you know, for money, [LAUGHS] you know? You know how we got them. And we got the vehicles. We got what we want. And we went step by step--

speaker: interviewer
text: We couldn't get gas. So we get benzine we got from vehicles what they are blowing up, or parts of some of them. They left them, you know, Russian, that. With-- with-- we cut up pipes, and we suck it out from the tanks, and we put in our tanks. You understand? Till we could go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got rid of them, you know, what we didn't have enough oil, parts got, Panzers. We couldn't fix them, so we left them, and we took horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many horses did you take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think six, about eight horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the-- and for how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: We tied them up, you know, with the wagons. It was-- it was no way to go with wagons. We just made branches-- some sick of us and food, we took in the branches with the horses. Some was just left to kill. They got wounded or-- you know, got broken legs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of there were you in your group, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were about 24, about 30. Who knows? Something like that. You know, in this kind of condition, you don't care. Believe me, I didn't know even the Jewish guys who were there from my own town. Everybody and his self. And he says, if you don't survive, forget it. No brother, no sister-- nobody. You take by yourself. Protect yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we got beautiful knives. You understand? And we got, we got weapons. How much we wanted, and what-- what kind. We choose what kind we wanted. And till we got into the river before Kiev. And this was the worst thing what we could get, is to go over. And go over, then we were already on Russian side. Still was already the Germans were in Kiev. But we got better chances to go deep to Russia, to pass the river. Somehow we passed the river, you know? Do you remember which river it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Kiev-- I-- [LAUGHS] I don't remember the name. Don't forget kids, and don't forget it's 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's understandable.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we went the river through A-1, you know? We-- we did a beautiful job. We got boats, you know, and we got through. And finally, we went in. About 20, 30 kilometer, we pass Kiev. We find in the bush a train. It was loaded with kids and older people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somehow, I don't know-- it was a back lane, like for industrial, or was a camp district. You understand? Not in the main lane. Was outside from the main lane about 20, 30 kilometer. Also was manufacturing their-- Like an industrial park.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me? Extension-- yeah. It was a little train, not from the huge ones. It was about 20 cattle-- you know, cattle wagons? And when we come in there, they didn't have food. We give them our own food, our food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went in to the commander. Everything was controlled by the army partisans. But all in uniform, you know? There were partisans that kind of were lost from the army, you know? What-- what-- they got lost from the army, you know? They create partisans, groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a commander, very nice guy, young guy. And he says, we got no room. We got the kids and older people. If you wanted your group, I take you with pleasure. We're leaving in a few hours. The only room you get is in roof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did he explain where these children were from?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't say nothing. There was no time to talk. You know, the-- the the-- the-- what do you call them? The-- the train [INTERPOSING VOICES] engine, what do you call them?

speaker: interviewer
text: The locomotive?

speaker: interviewee
text: The locomotive was in full swing, you know, and they're putting in water. And he says, we're going, you know, deep to Russia, to [RUSSIAN] in two nights. And if you want to go, you go on the roof. They help us up. He says, we give you string. We give you everything you can. And you have to be laid down, not sitting down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through bushes we go through, and fence-- you know, what do you call them-- eye-draw things, who knows what, you know, on the highways? And that's nights. If you want to survive, the only thing you have to tied up each other in the-- in the roof of the train. So we grabbed what we could. But weapons was no use to take with us, weapons. The only thing we grabbed-- we left everything. And the only thing to take with us is food, food and water.

speaker: interviewee
text: But water was how much you like. And the station's hard water. They call them [NON-ENGLISH]. We got-- it was boiling kettle. And who got tools got in the water. And food was no problem. We still got lots of food. And we jumped in in the rooftop And we tied up ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your whole group choose to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went for three days. Three or four days we were in the roof. Excuse me, no washroom, no nothing. What we got to eat we eat, and the train was going, you know, day to night, night and day was just noising to wake us up, to keep us alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we got into [SIGHS] to Russia. I don't know. [INAUDIBLE] or-- I don't know the names. And they took us. They took us to the station. They feed us. And they took our clothes away for-- we got lots-- excuse me-- lice. They took away our lice. They called them [NON-ENGLISH], to go and kill it in steam, you know? They explain us. We get our-- our clothes back, and they put us in Turkish baths, you know? And who were these people, the 'they'?

speaker: interviewee
text: The army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The army.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already the regional Russian volunteers, you know? Volunteers or army-- who knows? But they took us in like kings, and they give us what they can to feed us. And then, after they clean us up, they send us, each one, in a train coming in-- so many people, from us groups, and they send us to Bukhara.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't disconnect our group. The lieutenant didn't let our students-- didn't let to split us. So they sent us up to Bukhara. And they took us to the same university. They put us in there. You hear me? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school. But then this school was already for painting-- the academy of painting-- painters. Who care? But the funniest things is, I was going on my name. And they call us up to the office. I was scared to death. Who knows what I did-- what I didn't do?

speaker: interviewer
text: And they told us that it's Passover. From the Jewish Society in Bukhara, they invite us for Passover. Can you believe it? Stalin communists, what they claim? And they send us to the-- to families for two weeks for Passover. And they treat us like kings. You know, they cleaned us up. They give us, you know, food, good, and they kept us close to two weeks. Do you remember the name of the family that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. The only thing I know is a fat lady. She was the rabbi's representative. She was a doctor, and she was the rabbi's woman. She took us to the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the matzo, everything was homemade, more kosher like we are here, very kosher. They got the tallits-- everything-- prays, you know, like-- Bukhara. OK? How many Jewish students went?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of them were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about eight students.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight. The eight Jewish students that you started with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but one redhead, not from our city, got dysentery. You know, bleeding dysentery. There's no cure for it. I remember that in school. So they burned his room with him together, not to spread these bacteria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Bukhara.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I can swear for it. So then, [SIGHS] when we finished the courses, this-- me-- I-- with these guys, I don't know. They disappeared, the Jewish boys. When they-- when they finished the-- the university, they disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Each went for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what subjects did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent us to Magnitogorsk. I didn't-- I didn't want to play smart. They send us Magnitogorsk, to build [INAUDIBLE] to build the ovens for steel. You understand I'm talking? To make the tank steel, to produce. Like, we were finished engineering, so we come there, they treat us like kings. They give us underground bungalows, nice food, winter clothes, and they send us to build number two, number three, number four [INAUDIBLE]. That is the ovens for to make steel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we specialize in foundation. We got other groups-- older people, and they-- we-- they dig for us the bulldozers, and we put in the two-by-four, the wood, for the cement to pour for the foundation, and the steel bars. That's all what we did. And how large a group were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were lots-- lots. And there was lots adults. There was lots of adults. But our-- our boss was-- our leader, with a beard-- he was from the aristocrats. From the-- from the king, from the czar, from Russia, the aristocrat? He explain us who he is. He was in the jail, but when the war started, they let him in. He was an engineer from-- from Moscow. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he gave you his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- sure, you know? We lived together. And he told all his life story, what in jail, everything. Anyways, he was our leader. And when we build up these things, they told us they need chauffeurs for the frontier. They're going to open up jungles in Zlatoust. They're going to open up jungles for they need the wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I volunteer to be a chauffeur. That took a year. Before--

speaker: interviewee
text: School. No, no-- the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months theory and six months practice. And I got the-- I got with me the license, 924. Want me to show you the license?

speaker: interviewer
text: At the-- At the end. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I got the license. Then, in Russia, to have a license-- chauffeur license-- was like, here in Canada, Rothschild. Who was the richest man in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rockefeller.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rockefeller. OK, near of Rothschild. You got a passport to be a multibillionaire. You understand? So famous was a chauffeur. Why you can't get it. Like I told you, you have to go in a school there. Engineering, technique, then-- then you go practice a half year. And then you go to a test. When you work off on that test-- we were about 48 or 50 students. Just eight or 12 got the license from us, from the whole school. There's 12 people sitting there, and everybody's shooting. You're sitting in a chair like I sit here, with a white shirt, and questions right and left. Then you get out. And, after finish, then they tell you who passed and who didn't pass. The nicest thing I liked, that from the 12 people was the chief in KGB. I got the signature there. Can't-- can't deny it. And in the-- and the license. And when the kids, what we got the license, they took us in for a meeting. And we got juice there, you know, and beautiful pastry. You can't even get them for no money, even here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this school, Mr. Eizenstein?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city was it in-- what area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magnitogorsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're still in Magnitogorsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your leader that you had started out with still with you, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name? No. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very bad to remember the name. You know why? I deny everything. I tell you why-- I'm very good to remember, but I don't want it to remember. And I can't-- what I went through, unjustice, I denied everything. I just take what is good for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I want to know. Rest, I don't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, when-- when finished, he took us into the room. And it was beautiful served, you know? Soft drinks-- I mean, juices and pastry. I've still got the taste from the pastry. And he told us, boys, you get a license today. Tomorrow, I'm going to get them back. So he threatened. He was a little guy, a general. He was very rough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you know he did? So they send us-- in Russia like that, they called them MTS-- a station from trucks and cars. You come in the morning. It's written your name and what kind of vehicle and for who you are working for, every day for somebody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Organizations, you know, used to phone in, or-- I don't know-- to send in-- they need a truck for that or a car for that. That's the way in Russia it goes on [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know now. They call them MTS. It's like a base, like the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And each one-- like the bakery-- for the bakery, you know? For all these-- coal-- you know, for all services. You know, from the MTS, they supply vehicles. So in the morning, I come in to work. He was there. He calls me up-- he gives me a little this-- a half-ton truck. And this was in-- in coal, you know? We didn't have gas. Was on wood, wood and coal, the motors were working, not on benzine. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I start, and he says, he wanted the right to check me out driving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was shaking, you know? He took me to the city. In Russia, when a bus stops you can't pass a bus. You understand me? You have to stop, and then, when the bus start to go, then you can pass it or the tramway, things like that. Plus regulations, you know? You know, got signs. Anyway, so I passed that, and he give me the hand that says you're going to do good. And then they sent us away to Zlatoust. Before we go on to there, can you tell us a little bit about the daily life in Magnitogorsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: The daily life-- I'm not going to tell you the secrets. But I was there a multibillionaire. You know, we kept our money here. They told me-- they told me how to cut off a hole and put the rubles here. We didn't know what to do. Golden teeths. We were billionaires. You know, everything was payoff. You-- you understand me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Were you able to communicate or find out what was happening to your family back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and I could-- one day I come in. They give me a three-ton truck. I couldn't reach it. The steering, the steering was bigger for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they-- they got so sorry for me, and they brought me cushions, you know? And they give me a cushion to-- to put me higher to the steering. You understand me? Why? I couldn't reach the steering. And-- and that's the way it was. So [SIGHS] then, they say you're going to Zlatoust. Had you had any opportunity to celebrate any of the holidays the way you had in Bukhara?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't-- I didn't bother. But you say conditions? I give you an example. I never saw opera in my life, just there. Every weekend, they give us tickets to operas, movies. And it's not like here. You go in a movie, you play billiard, pinball-- all entertainment till you get, you know, if you come early, you know? And you got canteens there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, I mean, we're living like-- you know, people were dying from food or people-- you know, in the wartime, you know? We were living like kings. The only thing was, like-- we have to go to our homes to the tramway. You understand what a tramway is? Electric bus, you know? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was far away. This was outside the city, a village they built for us. But the inside was everything. You know, it was underground, but we were sleeping three-- three guys in each row.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we were-- you know, we got our clubs there, entertainment. It was not bad. You get-- the clothes was unbelievable good, warm clothes. The best clothes money can buy, you know? What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then they sent us-- the young guys what we finished, they send us to Zlatoust. That is, like, more or less severe, [SIGHS] in the jungle.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a jungle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. So the only jungle open is winter. When you look-- they build bridges. When you look down, you have to fall down. You get dizzy. And they give us fire. The whole trucks was-- they light it on fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, what-- before we go to jungle, I says, what is that? And they told me, the wolves are going to eat you up alive. So we have to keep fire on your trucks. And they still used to fall in-- wolves used to invade our trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we come in, deep in the jungle, about two hours' drive, were Cossacks and Uzbeks they brought in from Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan. And they build them homes from snow blocks. In the middle was fire from wood. So some-- if you lay on this side you're warm, and this side you freeze. So we say every day 10, 20 were freezing to death. In the morning you wake him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of you lived in these blocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't live. We just were chauffeurs. We come in-- our trailers were already loaded. We just come in. Hook them in, you turn, and you went to the city. In the city was a lake-- you know, frozen lake? And then, when summer comes, the wood goes-- the timber. But we was very danger. In-- in there-- the wheels-- you know, what eight-wheelers-- the wheels were made from timber-- from the timber. This was not in air, and the kind of air blows-- doesn't work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the worst thing was, in the morning you have to wake up, 4 o'clock, to heated up the engine differential, you know, with-- with oil and-- and what do you call them? Clothes, you know, and kerosene, and mix it up in pails. And you used to warm up the engines. And then you got hard water, after you warm up 13 minutes, you keep in. Then they give you boiling water. Threw boiling water on the engine, and then the radiator, to start it. And how many of these vehicles were you responsible for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just got-- each one got one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each one got one.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was too-- too weak to start it, you know? You know, engine like that, there's no batteries like you got now. You have to start it. I was crying, begging mine friends to help me, you know, to start it. Some of them didn't want to. They were busy with their own. I was in very bad condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was one season. And then I read in the paper-- I couldn't take it, on account I couldn't start the engines. Not that I didn't want it. It was beautiful. Not just that they give us private homes to live, board and room. The government paid for it. We were living very rich. And how many of you were in a home?

speaker: interviewee
text: One. Each--

speaker: interviewer
text: You each had your own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you each had your own home, and they get paid. Everybody was crazy to get us, on account they wood free and they got, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you boarded with people who lived in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: High class. High-class people. The food was unbelievable. And our clothes. And we made money. You know, why? I brought in-- with my truck, I  brought in one-- one tree. [LAUGHS] I was a millionaire to sell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people that you lived with know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that an issue?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was-- there were members Communist Party, you know? Is not a thing. They don't know even what it is, if you are Jewish. They don't-- they-- they don't know. Deep in Russia, they don't know at all what it is. They don't even mention. And-- and finally I find the one guy from [INAUDIBLE] was married. Had a girl, got already two kids. His father-in-law was a bush man, the manager from the bush. He was there before me. I don't know how he got in there. And he was married to a Gentile, you know, to a Russian woman. And he got two children. But he come. He Told me he's Jewish. Why are you new in the office, Eizenstein. And it was very good. But then I took in the paper one evening, and I saw they liberate my town. So I went into the army, and I says, I want to volunteer. And they say, first thing, you're too young, and second, we got passport-- our passport was with red-- not red, pink. Pink stripe, in the middle of the passport. All the pages. That mainly they can mobilize us. You know, if they catch us in the city or there, the-- you know, the police-- army police, you know-- I come [INAUDIBLE] you don't go. That was our password, you know, with a pink slip-- pink.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on one day, another day they didn't. So I says, you know what, excuse me, you know, I'm going myself. I took off. OK. And we'll continue.
